posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
William Kelley of Wine Advocate fame ,covering Burgundy, Chablis, Beaujolais, Champagne, has graciously offered to do a live Q&A session (AMA - Ask me anything!) for the holidays.

Now is your opportunity to ask him everything that’s been on your mind (about wine).

Since William will be in the US for the holidays, we’ve scheduled it for December 9th, 2020 at 5:30PST. We’ll post a zoom link when time comes.

How the Q&A will be handled

\

  1. All users can post questions in the zoom chat. Priority will be given to GCC members then to non GCC members. We will attempt to cross reference members, so please use your username/actual name on WB on Zoom.

  2. Questions will be asked by me or Todd to William. This will focus the time spent without misc chit chat or telling William what’s in your glass or thanking William for something.

  3. everyone will be on mute.

  4. we will post the link the day of the zoom. First 100 people in will be allowed in, first come first serve. We’ll admit as people join.

Please note - the zoom chat can only handle up to 100 people. So first come first serve. There is no sign up list. first come first serve once we post the link

Zoom link Launch Meeting - Zoom

posts.name: Ron Slye
posts.cooked:
Oh yes, on youtube the people there cannot ask questions directly (which helps with having too many questions!). Though they can post comments or questions in the youtube chat, but it can be clear that no one other than other watchers will respond.

If you do the youtube stream, you can still do the 100 person zoom stream. So the 100 people on zoom can ask questions, and the rest on youtube can watch and listen. In other words, you can do both.

But William raises a valid point – indirectly and cheekily of course! The “problem” with youtube is that it is then open to everyone, including those who are not members of WB. So that may be a concern if you want to limit this to people from this community. (I should say that I do not know whether you can limit the youtube stream with a password or something. We have never done that so I don’t know if it is even an option.)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Just to throw out a couple of themes / questions that interest me / that I’ve been thinking about a lot lately:

  • Contemporary white and red Burgundy winemaking styles (begging the question, what is true greatness in Chardonnay? and in Pinot Noir?)
  • What is the future of / challenges facing professional wine criticism?
  • Premature oxidation
  • How making wine can inform writing about wine
  • Climate change in Burgundy & Champagne

But happy to range as widely as people like!

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:

  • What is the future of / challenges facing professional wine criticism?
  • Premature oxidation

  • Climate change in Burgundy & Champagne
    - William’s personal Bingo Card for wine descriptors

But happy to range as widely as people like!

A suggested topic for John Morris’ entertainment.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
For those that can’t make it, we’ll post the video, sheesh! If we have enough demand, maybe we need to do a repeat, or I should raise the number of max attendees for one session. Then we’d have too many questions…might want to do this as a moderated video instead, where questions are submitted and vetted ahead of time?

If you and Charlie are asking the questions, watching live versus a recording is not materially different. Just collect questions in advance and save the money necessary to raise the maximum. I will give up my “spot” to someone who can’t wait a few hours to watch the recording if you do it that way.

Random other suggestions:

  1. Exclude any question that uses the word “Parker” in it because . . . well, just because . . . isn’t it obvious for so many reasons?

  2. Ask him about under the radar producers that we should look for.

  3. Ask him about his opinion of Beaujolais and whether the new waive of quality wine making is creating Beaujolais worth searching for, and if so, which ones.

  4. Ask him the inevitable question about what the H really causes premox.

  5. Ask him if the massive use of screw top closures for white wine in places like Austria will migrate to Burgundy and whether using screw top closures with oxygen permeability equal to cork is likely to allow aging without premox. BTW - This question deserves the WGOTY award. G=geek.

  6. Ask him what I can buy to find out if DRC is really worth the price without actually paying for DRC.

Contemporary white and red Burgundy winemaking styles (begging the question, what is true greatness in Chardonnay? and in Pinot Noir?)

I have really enjoyed your discussions here on this topic (esp. if you include farming as part of winemaking) and think this would make a fantastic topic. I keep reading and hearing about a new generation of Burgundy producers (and have been drinking some of these like from Clerget, Heitz-Lochardet and Hudelot-Noellat) and so am really interested in where Burgundy is going. How much new and how much back to their roots - anyone going to make wine like Jacky Truchot???

  • investment in equipment
  • winemaking methods
  • tasting room experiences
  • marketing, etc.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Just to throw out a couple of themes / questions that interest me / that I’ve been thinking about a lot lately:

  • Contemporary white and red Burgundy winemaking styles (begging the question, what is true greatness in Chardonnay? and in Pinot Noir?)
  • What is the future of / challenges facing professional wine criticism?
  • Premature oxidation
  • How making wine can inform writing about wine
  • Climate change in Burgundy & Champagne
    - William’s personal Bingo Card for wine descriptors

But happy to range as widely as people like!

A suggested topic for John Morris’ entertainment.

Do you really want to waste everyone’s time with THAT?

If we took that in the direction of “how can we describe wine in tasting notes in ways that are more meaningful” it could be interesting… including the problem of translating vs transliterating non-anglophone, and especially French, tasting terminology!

posts.name: Brad England
posts.cooked:
Great edition guys, thank you. William is knowledgable, articulate and refreshingly candid. Todd’s deep voice and charm are perfectly suited to the medium. And for many, I’m sure Charlie effortlessly encourages and reaffirms their sartorial and home decor apathies.
I resubscribed to WA last year solely due to William and don’t regret it.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
On the Bouley Pommard Fremiers, this is the location: Weinlagen. I don’t have the bottling, only his Pommard Rugiens. Fremiers seems to be right next to Volnay 1er cru les Fremiets: Weinlagen

I think people tend to fixate on the Rugiens, because it’s such a well known site. In fact, Thomas is in Rugiens Hauts, where the soils are white and calcareous (right above one of Clerget’s two parcels at the northern extreme of the climat). And it is a rather ethereal, fine-boned wine, not at all the sort of massive, rich, muscular stereotype of Rugiens that one finds realized in e.g. old bottles from Pothier-Rieusset, M Gaunoux etc. Chez Bouley, the Fremiers is the more complete and complex wine, though they are both great bottlings. It took me a few visits to understand this myself. We’re planning a vertical of Fremiers in the Spring so it will be getting a bit more publicity in the near term…

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked:
Just finished watching the tape. Lot of knowledge (obv) and energy from William. Appreciated the directness of many of his comments and his willingness to share his thoughts. I had to smile at the correlation between the clothing, the background setting, and conversational manner of the three of you gentlemen. Thanks to all, especially William. Cheers.

P.S. William, how does a wine bottle earn a display on the bookshelf? Also are you a book collector? Thank you. -Jim

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Just finished watching the tape. Lot of knowledge (obv) and energy from William. Appreciated the directness of many of his comments and his willingness to share his thoughts. I had to smile at the correlation between the clothing, the background setting, and conversational manner of the three of you gentlemen. Thanks to all, especially William. Cheers.

P.S. William, how does a wine bottle earn a display on the bookshelf? Also are you a book collector? Thank you. -Jim

Thanks for the kind words, Jim! Only just saw this. The bottles on the bookshelf are just nice recently opened bottles that survived recycling: the more competitive spot is the mantelpiece in Beaune, where, in the interests of matrimonial harmony, I am obliged to remove one bottle for every one that I add. My wife holds me to this rule quite strictly, to the extent of once recycling several empty Gentaz bottles while I was traveling in the Far East.

I would say I buy books rather than collecting them. While I appreciate nice bindings and editions, I don’t really own any books that aren’t readable copies, purchased to read or for reference. I do have a few books that belonged to the likes of John Ruskin, William Gladstone etc, acquired during my days studying 19th-century history, which are something of an exception to that rule, but for the most part mine is an entirely functional library. Just as the bottles in my cellar are intended to be opened!
